slides.headline: You got the offer.
slides.eyebrow: Education · Sync deal checklist — what to negotiate
fit_warnings: [01 hook.eyebrow] copy (51 chars) exceeds slot budget (48 at 44px) — needs a rewrite

slides.body: Your first instinct is to say yes immediately.
Here's why that's the most expensive reaction in sync. ↓
When a placement offer lands, you have exactly one negotiation window. Before you sign, here's what experienced composers always check:

THE UPFRONT FEE
The first number is an opening bid — not the final price.
For branded content: fees typically run 20–40% higher than the first offer.
For streaming originals: push for the stated upper range.
Ask once. Clearly. Most supervisors expect it.
fit_warnings: [05 context.body] copy (250 chars) exceeds slot budget (206 at 48px) — needs a rewrite

slides.body:
EXCLUSIVITY PERIOD
"Exclusive" locks your track for a window — no other placements during that time.
Six months: acceptable.
Two years: 3× the standard rate.
Negotiate the window before you negotiate the fee. TERRITORY
UK-only and global use are fundamentally different deals.
A global campaign should cost 3–5× a regional one.
If the territory isn't defined in the offer: define it before you respond. TERM LENGTH
One-off vs. rolling vs. in-perpetuity are three different contracts.
In-perpetuity means they can use your track forever.
That has a price significantly above the standard placement fee. PERFORMANCE ROYALTIES
These are separate from your upfront fee.
Every time the content airs anywhere, royalties generate through your PRO.
Non-negotiable prerequisite: register before any placement is signed.
You cannot collect these retroactively. The sync fee is the headline number.
Performance royalties are the compounding income beneath it. Know both before you sign anything. #SyncDeal #AfricanComposers #SyncLicensing #MusicBusiness #SyncMaster
